issue.message: Call requires API level 23 (current min is 21): `android.webkit.WebResourceError#getErrorCode`
issue.summary: Calling new methods on older versions
issue.explanation:
This check scans through all the Android API calls in the application and warns about any calls that are not available on **all** versions targeted by this application (according to its minimum SDK attribute in the manifest).
If you really want to use this API and don't need to support older devices just set the `minSdkVersion` in your `build.gradle` or `AndroidManifest.xml` files.
If your code is **deliberately** accessing newer APIs, and you have ensured (e.g. with conditional execution) that this code will only ever be called on a supported platform, then you can annotate your class or method with the `@TargetApi` annotation specifying the local minimum SDK to apply, such as `@TargetApi(11)`, such that this check considers 11 rather than your manifest file's minimum SDK as the required API level.
If you are deliberately setting `android:` attributes in style definitions, make sure you place this in a `values-v`*NN* folder in order to avoid running into runtime conflicts on certain devices where manufacturers have added custom attributes whose ids conflict with the new ones on later platforms.
Similarly, you can use tools:targetApi="11" in an XML file to indicate that the element will only be inflated in an adequate context.

issue.message: Call requires API level 23 (current min is 21): `android.webkit.WebResourceError#getErrorCode`
issue.summary: Calling new methods on older versions
issue.explanation:
This check scans through all the Android API calls in the application and warns about any calls that are not available on **all** versions targeted by this application (according to its minimum SDK attribute in the manifest).
If you really want to use this API and don't need to support older devices just set the `minSdkVersion` in your `build.gradle` or `AndroidManifest.xml` files.
If your code is **deliberately** accessing newer APIs, and you have ensured (e.g. with conditional execution) that this code will only ever be called on a supported platform, then you can annotate your class or method with the `@TargetApi` annotation specifying the local minimum SDK to apply, such as `@TargetApi(11)`, such that this check considers 11 rather than your manifest file's minimum SDK as the required API level.
If you are deliberately setting `android:` attributes in style definitions, make sure you place this in a `values-v`*NN* folder in order to avoid running into runtime conflicts on certain devices where manufacturers have added custom attributes whose ids conflict with the new ones on later platforms.
Similarly, you can use tools:targetApi="11" in an XML file to indicate that the element will only be inflated in an adequate context.

issue.message: Call requires API level 23 (current min is 21): `android.webkit.WebResourceError#getDescription`
issue.summary: Calling new methods on older versions
issue.explanation:
This check scans through all the Android API calls in the application and warns about any calls that are not available on **all** versions targeted by this application (according to its minimum SDK attribute in the manifest).
If you really want to use this API and don't need to support older devices just set the `minSdkVersion` in your `build.gradle` or `AndroidManifest.xml` files.
If your code is **deliberately** accessing newer APIs, and you have ensured (e.g. with conditional execution) that this code will only ever be called on a supported platform, then you can annotate your class or method with the `@TargetApi` annotation specifying the local minimum SDK to apply, such as `@TargetApi(11)`, such that this check considers 11 rather than your manifest file's minimum SDK as the required API level.
If you are deliberately setting `android:` attributes in style definitions, make sure you place this in a `values-v`*NN* folder in order to avoid running into runtime conflicts on certain devices where manufacturers have added custom attributes whose ids conflict with the new ones on later platforms.
Similarly, you can use tools:targetApi="11" in an XML file to indicate that the element will only be inflated in an adequate context.

issue.explanation:
This check scans through all the Android API calls in the application and warns about any calls that are not available on **all** versions targeted by this application (according to its minimum SDK attribute in the manifest).
If you really want to use this API and don't need to support older devices just set the `minSdkVersion` in your `build.gradle` or `AndroidManifest.xml` files.
If your code is **deliberately** accessing newer APIs, and you have ensured (e.g. with conditional execution) that this code will only ever be called on a supported platform, then you can annotate your class or method with the `@TargetApi` annotation specifying the local minimum SDK to apply, such as `@TargetApi(11)`, such that this check considers 11 rather than your manifest file's minimum SDK as the required API level.
If you are deliberately setting `android:` attributes in style definitions, make sure you place this in a `values-v`*NN* folder in order to avoid running into runtime conflicts on certain devices where manufacturers have added custom attributes whose ids conflict with the new ones on later platforms.
Similarly, you can use tools:targetApi="11" in an XML file to indicate that the element will only be inflated in an adequate context.

issue.summary: Calling new methods on older versions
issue.explanation:
This check scans through all the Android API calls in the application and warns about any calls that are not available on **all** versions targeted by this application (according to its minimum SDK attribute in the manifest).
If you really want to use this API and don't need to support older devices just set the `minSdkVersion` in your `build.gradle` or `AndroidManifest.xml` files.
If your code is **deliberately** accessing newer APIs, and you have ensured (e.g. with conditional execution) that this code will only ever be called on a supported platform, then you can annotate your class or method with the `@TargetApi` annotation specifying the local minimum SDK to apply, such as `@TargetApi(11)`, such that this check considers 11 rather than your manifest file's minimum SDK as the required API level.
If you are deliberately setting `android:` attributes in style definitions, make sure you place this in a `values-v`*NN* folder in order to avoid running into runtime conflicts on certain devices where manufacturers have added custom attributes whose ids conflict with the new ones on later platforms.
Similarly, you can use tools:targetApi="11" in an XML file to indicate that the element will only be inflated in an adequate context.

issue.message: Call requires API level 23 (current min is 21): `android.webkit.WebResourceError#getDescription`
issue.summary: Calling new methods on older versions
issue.explanation:
This check scans through all the Android API calls in the application and warns about any calls that are not available on **all** versions targeted by this application (according to its minimum SDK attribute in the manifest).
If you really want to use this API and don't need to support older devices just set the `minSdkVersion` in your `build.gradle` or `AndroidManifest.xml` files.
If your code is **deliberately** accessing newer APIs, and you have ensured (e.g. with conditional execution) that this code will only ever be called on a supported platform, then you can annotate your class or method with the `@TargetApi` annotation specifying the local minimum SDK to apply, such as `@TargetApi(11)`, such that this check considers 11 rather than your manifest file's minimum SDK as the required API level.
If you are deliberately setting `android:` attributes in style definitions, make sure you place this in a `values-v`*NN* folder in order to avoid running into runtime conflicts on certain devices where manufacturers have added custom attributes whose ids conflict with the new ones on later platforms.
Similarly, you can use tools:targetApi="11" in an XML file to indicate that the element will only be inflated in an adequate context.

issue.message: Not targeting the latest versions of Android; compatibility modes apply. Consider testing and updating this version. Consult the `android.os.Build.VERSION_CODES` javadoc for details.
issue.summary: Target SDK attribute is not targeting latest version
issue.explanation:
When your application runs on a version of Android that is more recent than your `targetSdkVersion` specifies that it has been tested with, various compatibility modes kick in. This ensures that your application continues to work, but it may look out of place. For example, if the `targetSdkVersion` is less than 14, your app may get an option button in the UI.
To fix this issue, set the `targetSdkVersion` to the highest available value. Then test your app to make sure everything works correctly. You may want to consult the compatibility notes to see what changes apply to each version you are adding support for: https://developer.android.com/reference/android/os/Build.VERSION_CODES.html as well as follow this guide: https://developer.android.com/distribute/best-practices/develop/target-sdk.html

issue.message: Attribute `usesCleartextTraffic` is only used in API level 23 and higher (current min is 21)
issue.explanation:
This check finds attributes set in XML files that were introduced in a version newer than the oldest version targeted by your application (with the `minSdkVersion` attribute).
This is not an error; the application will simply ignore the attribute. However, if the attribute is important to the appearance or functionality of your application, you should consider finding an alternative way to achieve the same result with only available attributes, and then you can optionally create a copy of the layout in a layout-vNN folder which will be used on API NN or higher where you can take advantage of the newer attribute.
Note: This check does not only apply to attributes. For example, some tags can be unused too, such as the new `<tag>` element in layouts introduced in API 21.

issue.message: Attribute `networkSecurityConfig` is only used in API level 24 and higher (current min is 21)
issue.explanation:
This check finds attributes set in XML files that were introduced in a version newer than the oldest version targeted by your application (with the `minSdkVersion` attribute).
This is not an error; the application will simply ignore the attribute. However, if the attribute is important to the appearance or functionality of your application, you should consider finding an alternative way to achieve the same result with only available attributes, and then you can optionally create a copy of the layout in a layout-vNN folder which will be used on API NN or higher where you can take advantage of the newer attribute.
Note: This check does not only apply to attributes. For example, some tags can be unused too, such as the new `<tag>` element in layouts introduced in API 21.

issue.message: A newer version of androidx.core:core-ktx than 1.13.1 is available: 1.18.0
issue.explanation: This detector looks for usages of libraries where the version you are using is not the current stable release. Using older versions is fine, and there are cases where you deliberately want to stick with an older version. However, you may simply not be aware that a more recent version is available, and that is what this lint check helps find.

issue.message: A newer version of androidx.appcompat:appcompat than 1.7.0 is available: 1.7.1
issue.explanation: This detector looks for usages of libraries where the version you are using is not the current stable release. Using older versions is fine, and there are cases where you deliberately want to stick with an older version. However, you may simply not be aware that a more recent version is available, and that is what this lint check helps find.

issue.message: A newer version of com.google.android.material:material than 1.12.0 is available: 1.13.0
issue.explanation: This detector looks for usages of libraries where the version you are using is not the current stable release. Using older versions is fine, and there are cases where you deliberately want to stick with an older version. However, you may simply not be aware that a more recent version is available, and that is what this lint check helps find.

issue.message: A newer version of androidx.constraintlayout:constraintlayout than 2.1.4 is available: 2.2.1
issue.explanation: This detector looks for usages of libraries where the version you are using is not the current stable release. Using older versions is fine, and there are cases where you deliberately want to stick with an older version. However, you may simply not be aware that a more recent version is available, and that is what this lint check helps find.

issue.explanation: This detector looks for usages of libraries where the version you are using is not the current stable release. Using older versions is fine, and there are cases where you deliberately want to stick with an older version. However, you may simply not be aware that a more recent version is available, and that is what this lint check helps find.

issue.message: A newer version of androidx.test.espresso:espresso-core than 3.6.1 is available: 3.7.0
issue.explanation: This detector looks for usages of libraries where the version you are using is not the current stable release. Using older versions is fine, and there are cases where you deliberately want to stick with an older version. However, you may simply not be aware that a more recent version is available, and that is what this lint check helps find.

issue.message: Permitting connections with SSL-related errors could allow eavesdroppers to intercept data sent by your app, which impacts the privacy of your users. Consider canceling the connections by invoking `SslErrorHandler#cancel()`.
issue.summary: Proceeds with the HTTPS connection despite SSL errors
issue.explanation: This check looks for `onReceivedSslError` implementations that invoke `SslErrorHandler#proceed`.

issue.message: Permitting connections with SSL-related errors could allow eavesdroppers to intercept data sent by your app, which impacts the privacy of your users. Consider canceling the connections by invoking `SslErrorHandler#cancel()`.
issue.summary: Proceeds with the HTTPS connection despite SSL errors
issue.explanation: This check looks for `onReceivedSslError` implementations that invoke `SslErrorHandler#proceed`.

issue.message: Unnecessary; SDK_INT is always >= 21
issue.explanation:
This check flags version checks that are not necessary, because the `minSdkVersion` (or surrounding known API level) is already at least as high as the version checked for.
Similarly, it also looks for resources in `-vNN` folders, such as `values-v14` where the version qualifier is less than or equal to the `minSdkVersion`, where the contents should be merged into the best folder.

issue.message: Unnecessary; SDK_INT is always >= 21
issue.explanation:
This check flags version checks that are not necessary, because the `minSdkVersion` (or surrounding known API level) is already at least as high as the version checked for.
Similarly, it also looks for resources in `-vNN` folders, such as `values-v14` where the version qualifier is less than or equal to the `minSdkVersion`, where the contents should be merged into the best folder.

issue.message: Should explicitly set `android:icon`, there is no default
issue.explanation: You should set an icon for the application as whole because there is no default. This attribute must be set as a reference to a drawable resource containing the image (for example `@drawable/icon`).

issue.message: Buttons in button bars should be borderless; use `style="?android:attr/buttonBarButtonStyle"` (and `?android:attr/buttonBarStyle` on the parent)
issue.explanation: Button bars typically use a borderless style for the buttons. Set the `style="?android:attr/buttonBarButtonStyle"` attribute on each of the buttons, and set `style="?android:attr/buttonBarStyle"` on the parent layout

issue.message: Buttons in button bars should be borderless; use `style="?android:attr/buttonBarButtonStyle"` (and `?android:attr/buttonBarStyle` on the parent)
issue.explanation: Button bars typically use a borderless style for the buttons. Set the `style="?android:attr/buttonBarButtonStyle"` attribute on each of the buttons, and set `style="?android:attr/buttonBarStyle"` on the parent layout

issue.explanation: Button bars typically use a borderless style for the buttons. Set the `style="?android:attr/buttonBarButtonStyle"` attribute on each of the buttons, and set `style="?android:attr/buttonBarStyle"` on the parent layout

issue.explanation: Button bars typically use a borderless style for the buttons. Set the `style="?android:attr/buttonBarButtonStyle"` attribute on each of the buttons, and set `style="?android:attr/buttonBarStyle"` on the parent layout

issue.message: Missing `autofillHints` attribute
issue.explanation:
Specify an `autofillHints` attribute when targeting SDK version 26 or higher or explicitly specify that the view is not important for autofill. Your app can help an autofill service classify the data correctly by providing the meaning of each view that could be autofillable, such as views representing usernames, passwords, credit card fields, email addresses, etc.
The hints can have any value, but it is recommended to use predefined values like 'username' for a username or 'creditCardNumber' for a credit card number. For a list of all predefined autofill hint constants, see the `AUTOFILL_HINT_` constants in the `View` reference at https://developer.android.com/reference/android/view/View.html.
You can mark a view unimportant for autofill by specifying an `importantForAutofill` attribute on that view or a parent view. See https://developer.android.com/reference/android/view/View.html#setImportantForAutofill(int).

issue.message: Missing `autofillHints` attribute
issue.explanation:
Specify an `autofillHints` attribute when targeting SDK version 26 or higher or explicitly specify that the view is not important for autofill. Your app can help an autofill service classify the data correctly by providing the meaning of each view that could be autofillable, such as views representing usernames, passwords, credit card fields, email addresses, etc.
The hints can have any value, but it is recommended to use predefined values like 'username' for a username or 'creditCardNumber' for a credit card number. For a list of all predefined autofill hint constants, see the `AUTOFILL_HINT_` constants in the `View` reference at https://developer.android.com/reference/android/view/View.html.
You can mark a view unimportant for autofill by specifying an `importantForAutofill` attribute on that view or a parent view. See https://developer.android.com/reference/android/view/View.html#setImportantForAutofill(int).

The hints can have any value, but it is recommended to use predefined values like 'username' for a username or 'creditCardNumber' for a credit card number. For a list of all predefined autofill hint constants, see the `AUTOFILL_HINT_` constants in the `View` reference at https://developer.android.com/reference/android/view/View.html.
You can mark a view unimportant for autofill by specifying an `importantForAutofill` attribute on that view or a parent view. See https://developer.android.com/reference/android/view/View.html#setImportantForAutofill(int).

issue.explanation:
Specify an `autofillHints` attribute when targeting SDK version 26 or higher or explicitly specify that the view is not important for autofill. The hints can have any value, but it is recommended to use predefined values like 'username' for a username or 'creditCardNumber' for a credit card number. For a list of all predefined autofill hint constants, see the `AUTOFILL_HINT_` constants in the `View` reference at https://developer.android.com/reference/android/view/View.html.
You can mark a view unimportant for autofill by specifying an `importantForAutofill` attribute on that view or a parent view. See https://developer.android.com/reference/android/view/View.html#setImportantForAutofill(int).

issue.message: Missing `autofillHints` attribute
issue.explanation:
Specify an `autofillHints` attribute when targeting SDK version 26 or higher or explicitly specify that the view is not important for autofill. Your app can help an autofill service classify the data correctly by providing the meaning of each view that could be autofillable, such as views representing usernames, passwords, credit card fields, email addresses, etc.
The hints can have any value, but it is recommended to use predefined values like 'username' for a username or 'creditCardNumber' for a credit card number. For a list of all predefined autofill hint constants, see the `AUTOFILL_HINT_` constants in the `View` reference at https://developer.android.com/reference/android/view/View.html.
You can mark a view unimportant for autofill by specifying an `importantForAutofill` attribute on that view or a parent view. See https://developer.android.com/reference/android/view/View.html#setImportantForAutofill(int).

issue.message: Missing `autofillHints` attribute
issue.explanation:
Specify an `autofillHints` attribute when targeting SDK version 26 or higher or explicitly specify that the view is not important for autofill. Your app can help an autofill service classify the data correctly by providing the meaning of each view that could be autofillable, such as views representing usernames, passwords, credit card fields, email addresses, etc.
The hints can have any value, but it is recommended to use predefined values like 'username' for a username or 'creditCardNumber' for a credit card number. For a list of all predefined autofill hint constants, see the `AUTOFILL_HINT_` constants in the `View` reference at https://developer.android.com/reference/android/view/View.html.
You can mark a view unimportant for autofill by specifying an `importantForAutofill` attribute on that view or a parent view. See https://developer.android.com/reference/android/view/View.html#setImportantForAutofill(int).

issue.message: Do not concatenate text displayed with `setText`. Use resource string with placeholders.
issue.explanation: When calling `TextView#setText` * Never call `Number#toString()` to format numbers; it will not handle fraction separators and locale-specific digits properly. Consider using `String#format` with proper format specifications (`%d` or `%f`) instead. * Do not pass a string literal (e.g. "Hello") to display text. Hardcoded text can not be properly translated to other languages. Consider using Android resource strings instead. * Do not build messages by concatenating text chunks. Such messages can not be properly translated.

issue.message: String literal in `setText` can not be translated. Use Android resources instead.
issue.explanation: When calling `TextView#setText` * Never call `Number#toString()` to format numbers; it will not handle fraction separators and locale-specific digits properly. Consider using `String#format` with proper format specifications (`%d` or `%f`) instead. * Do not pass a string literal (e.g. "Hello") to display text. Hardcoded text can not be properly translated to other languages. Consider using Android resource strings instead. * Do not build messages by concatenating text chunks. Such messages can not be properly translated.

issue.message: String literal in `setText` can not be translated. Use Android resources instead.
issue.explanation: When calling `TextView#setText` * Never call `Number#toString()` to format numbers; it will not handle fraction separators and locale-specific digits properly. Consider using `String#format` with proper format specifications (`%d` or `%f`) instead. * Do not pass a string literal (e.g. "Hello") to display text. Hardcoded text can not be properly translated to other languages. Consider using Android resource strings instead. * Do not build messages by concatenating text chunks. Such messages can not be properly translated.

issue.message: Number formatting does not take into account locale settings. Consider using `String.format` instead.
issue.explanation: When calling `TextView#setText` * Never call `Number#toString()` to format numbers; it will not handle fraction separators and locale-specific digits properly. Consider using `String#format` with proper format specifications (`%d` or `%f`) instead. * Do not pass a string literal (e.g. "Hello") to display text. issue.explanation: When calling `TextView#setText` * Never call `Number#toString()` to format numbers; it will not handle fraction separators and locale-specific digits properly. Consider using `String#format` with proper format specifications (`%d` or `%f`) instead. * Do not pass a string literal (e.g. "Hello") to display text. Hardcoded text can not be properly translated to other languages. Consider using Android resource strings instead. * Do not build messages by concatenating text chunks. Such messages can not be properly translated.

issue.message: Hardcoded string "Navegador", should use `@string` resource
issue.explanation:
Hardcoding text attributes directly in layout files is bad for several reasons:
* When creating configuration variations (for example for landscape or portrait) you have to repeat the actual text (and keep it up to date when making changes)
* The application cannot be translated to other languages by just adding new translations for existing string resources.
There are quickfixes to automatically extract this hardcoded string into a resource lookup.

issue.explanation:
Hardcoding text attributes directly in layout files is bad for several reasons:
* When creating configuration variations (for example for landscape or portrait) you have to repeat the actual text (and keep it up to date when making changes)
* The application cannot be translated to other languages by just adding new translations for existing string resources.
There are quickfixes to automatically extract this hardcoded string into a resource lookup.

Hardcoding text attributes directly in layout files is bad for several reasons:
* When creating configuration variations (for example for landscape or portrait) you have to repeat the actual text (and keep it up to date when making changes)
* The application cannot be translated to other languages by just adding new translations for existing string resources.
There are quickfixes to automatically extract this hardcoded string into a resource lookup.

issue.message: Hardcoded string "Estado: Desconectado", should use `@string` resource
issue.explanation:
Hardcoding text attributes directly in layout files is bad for several reasons:
* When creating configuration variations (for example for landscape or portrait) you have to repeat the actual text (and keep it up to date when making changes)
* The application cannot be translated to other languages by just adding new translations for existing string resources.
There are quickfixes to automatically extract this hardcoded string into a resource lookup.

issue.message: Hardcoded string "ID: (no registrado)", should use `@string` resource
issue.explanation:
Hardcoding text attributes directly in layout files is bad for several reasons:
* When creating configuration variations (for example for landscape or portrait) you have to repeat the actual text (and keep it up to date when making changes)
* The application cannot be translated to other languages by just adding new translations for existing string resources.
There are quickfixes to automatically extract this hardcoded string into a resource lookup.

* The application cannot be translated to other languages by just adding new translations for existing string resources.

Hardcoding text attributes directly in layout files is bad for several reasons:
* When creating configuration variations (for example for landscape or portrait) you have to repeat the actual text (and keep it up to date when making changes)
* The application cannot be translated to other languages by just adding new translations for existing string resources.

Hardcoding text attributes directly in layout files is bad for several reasons:
* When creating configuration variations (for example for landscape or portrait) you have to repeat the actual text (and keep it up to date when making changes)
* The application cannot be translated to other languages by just adding new translations for existing string resources.
There are quickfixes to automatically extract this hardcoded string into a resource lookup.
